class_schedule.topic: Mobile Learning: Definitions, Affordances, and Frameworks
learning_objectives: Define mobile learning and its unique affordances for education
Apply the FRAME model to analyze mobile learning contexts
Distinguish between mobile-assisted learning and mobile-native learning design
readings: Crompton, H. - A Historical Overview of M-Learning
Koole, M. - A Model for Framing Mobile Learning (FRAME)

lecture_notes.introduction: Mobile learning is not just e-learning on a smaller screen. Mobile devices enable fundamentally different learning experiences: context-sensitive, location-aware, socially connected, and available at the point of need.
key_concepts: Mobile affordances: portability, context-sensitivity, connectivity, personalization
FRAME model: device, learner, social aspects and their intersections
Microlearning: bite-sized content for just-in-time delivery
Mobile-first vs. responsive design approaches
Bring Your Own Device (BYOD) challenges and policies
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mobile affordance audit: use your own phone to access three educational resources and evaluate how each leverages (or fails to leverage) mobile-specific affordances

review.value: Koole's Framework for the Rational Analysis of Mobile Education considers three intersecting aspects: device (physical characteristics, capabilities), learner (prior knowledge, motivation), and social (communication, collaboration norms)
True mobile learning leverages what only mobile can do: GPS for location-based learning, camera for data collection, accelerometer for physical activity, push notifications for spaced practice

class_schedule.topic: Designing for Small Screens and Touch Interaction
learning_objectives: Apply mobile UX design principles to educational content
Design touch-friendly interfaces with appropriate target sizes
Optimize content presentation for varying screen sizes and orientations
readings: Apple Human Interface Guidelines: Mobile (selected sections)

lecture_notes.introduction: The constraints of mobile devices — small screens, touch input, variable connectivity, distraction-rich environments — require fundamentally different design approaches than desktop e-learning.
key_concepts: Mobile-first design philosophy: start with constraints
Touch target sizes: minimum 44x44 points (Apple) / 48x48 dp (Android)
Content prioritization: progressive disclosure on small screens
Responsive design: fluid grids, flexible images, media queries
Thumb zones: the natural reach area for one-handed phone use
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mobile redesign exercise: take a desktop e-learning page and redesign it for mobile-first, focusing on content prioritization, touch targets, and progressive disclosure
setup_requirements: Desktop e-learning page screenshot for redesign
Mobile wireframe templates or prototyping tool (Figma)

review.value: Show only the essential content initially; provide access to details through expandable sections, links, or sequential screens. Reduces cognitive overload on small screens.
Wroblewski's principle: designing for mobile constraints forces you to prioritize what matters most, resulting in better experiences on all devices

learning_objectives: Design microlearning modules optimized for mobile delivery
Apply spaced practice and retrieval practice using mobile push notifications
Evaluate when microlearning is and is not appropriate
readings: Kapp, K. & Defelice, R. - Microlearning: Short and Sweet Ch. 1-3
Bjork, R. - Desirable Difficulties in Learning

lecture_notes.introduction: Microlearning delivers focused content in 3-7 minute bursts, ideal for mobile consumption. When combined with spaced repetition and retrieval practice, it leverages both the mobile form factor and the science of memory.
key_concepts: Microlearning: single-objective, self-contained, 3-7 minutes
Spaced practice: distributing learning over time for retention
Retrieval practice: testing as a learning event, not just assessment
Push notification learning: time-delayed prompts for spaced retrieval
When NOT to use microlearning: complex skills, contextual knowledge, deep analysis
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design a 5-day microlearning campaign for onboarding new employees: create 5 micro-modules with spaced retrieval questions, specifying push notification timing

review.value: Distributing practice over days or weeks produces 10-30% better long-term retention than massing the same practice into a single session (hundreds of studies, robust effect)
Microlearning works for factual knowledge, procedures, and skill refreshers. It is NOT suitable for complex problem-solving, nuanced discussion, or skills requiring extended practice.

learning_objectives: Design location-based learning experiences using GPS and geofencing
Evaluate augmented reality tools for educational applications
Create a mobile learning experience that leverages physical context
readings: Dunleavy, M. & Dede, C. - Augmented Reality Teaching and Learning
Squire, K. - Mobile Media Learning Ch. 4

lecture_notes.introduction: Mobile devices know where they are. This opens entirely new categories of learning experiences: field trips that respond to location, augmented reality that overlays information on the real world, and context-sensitive learning that adapts to the learner's environment.
key_concepts: GPS-based learning trails and scavenger hunts
Geofencing: triggering content based on physical location
AR in education: marker-based, markerless, and location-based AR
Tools: ARIS, Actionbound, Google Lens, Merge Cube
Design considerations: physical safety, accessibility, weather, device requirements
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design a campus-based AR learning trail: identify 5 locations, create content triggers, and specify what learners see/do at each stop
setup_requirements: Actionbound or ARIS account for prototyping

review.value: Location-based mobile learning embodies Lave & Wenger's situated learning theory: knowledge is constructed in the context where it will be used, making transfer more natural
Effective educational AR adds information that is impossible to see otherwise (historical overlay, molecular structure), not decoration that could be shown on a screen

learning_objectives: Build a functional mobile learning app using no-code tools
Apply instructional design principles within app development constraints
Design for offline access and intermittent connectivity
readings: Overview of no-code tools: Glide, Thunkable, AppSheet

lecture_notes.introduction: You do not need to be a programmer to create mobile learning experiences. No-code and low-code platforms allow educational technologists to rapidly prototype and deploy mobile apps that serve real learning needs.
key_concepts: No-code platforms: Glide (spreadsheet-to-app), Thunkable (visual programming), AppSheet
Progressive web apps (PWAs): web apps that behave like native apps
Offline-first design: caching content for intermittent connectivity
App store vs. web app distribution trade-offs
Analytics integration: tracking learner behavior in mobile apps
lecture_notes.demonstration: Rapid app build: create a flashcard-based microlearning app using Glide from a Google Sheets data source in 30 minutes
setup_requirements: Pre-prepared Google Sheet with learning content

review.value: Transforms Google Sheets into functional mobile apps with no coding; ideal for content-driven educational apps, field data collection, and resource libraries
Design mobile learning content to work without connectivity and sync when a connection is available; critical for rural areas, developing countries, and field-based learning

class_schedule.topic: Mobile Learning in Global and Developing Contexts
learning_objectives: Analyze mobile learning initiatives in developing countries
Design mobile learning solutions for low-resource environments
Evaluate the equity implications of mobile-dependent education
readings: UNESCO - Policy Guidelines for Mobile Learning
Aker, J. & Mbiti, I. - Mobile Phones and Economic Development in Africa

lecture_notes.introduction: In much of the developing world, a mobile phone is the primary and sometimes only computing device available. Mobile learning is not a convenience in these contexts — it is the entire digital education infrastructure.
key_concepts: Mobile penetration in Sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, and Southeast Asia
Feature phone vs. smartphone learning: SMS-based and USSD-based education
Data cost sensitivity: designing for zero-rated or ultra-low bandwidth
UNESCO recommendations for mobile learning policy
Equity concerns: gender gaps in phone ownership and digital skills
lecture_notes.demonstration: Low-bandwidth design challenge: design a mobile learning experience that works on a feature phone using only SMS, delivering a 5-day learning sequence on a health topic
setup_requirements: Case studies of mobile learning in developing countries

review.value: Programs like Eneza Education and M-Shule in Kenya deliver quiz-based learning via SMS to millions of students on basic phones, demonstrating that mobile learning does not require smartphones or apps

In South Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa, women are 20-30% less likely than men to own a smartphone, which means mobile learning policies can inadvertently widen gender gaps without intentional equity measures

questions.question: An organization converts its 60-minute desktop e-learning modules into mobile-compatible PDFs. Why does this NOT constitute effective mobile learning? Using the FRAME model, identify what is missing.
questions.answer: Converting desktop PDFs to mobile merely shrinks the format without leveraging mobile affordances. Using the FRAME model: the Device aspect is addressed (content is viewable on phones) but the intersection of Device and Learner (mobile-optimized interaction, microlearning chunks, touch-friendly navigation) is missing. The Social aspect (collaborative features, sharing, real-time communication) is entirely absent. And the intersection of all three aspects (meaningful mobile learning) is not achieved because the design does not use mobile-specific affordances: location-awareness, push notifications for spaced practice, camera for authentic tasks, or context-sensitivity.

rubric.key: Explains why format conversion is insufficient
Applies FRAME model to identify missing aspects
Identifies specific mobile affordances that should be leveraged

questions.question: Under what conditions is microlearning NOT an appropriate instructional strategy? Give two specific examples with justification.
questions.answer: Microlearning is inappropriate when: (1) The learning objective requires sustained, complex problem-solving — e.g., designing a database schema requires holding multiple interrelated constraints in working memory simultaneously, which cannot be effectively chunked into 3-minute segments without losing the systemic connections. (2) The content requires deep discussion and multiple perspectives — e.g., analyzing the ethics of a medical case study requires reading nuanced arguments, considering multiple stakeholder perspectives, and constructing a reasoned position, which needs extended engagement time and social dialogue, not a micro-module.

rubric.key: First example with clear justification for why microlearning fails
Second example with clear justification for why microlearning fails
Examples are genuinely distinct scenarios, not variations

quizzes.topic: AR, App Development, and Global Contexts

questions.question: What distinguishes effective educational augmented reality from AR that is merely novel? Describe one design principle and illustrate it with an example.
questions.answer: Effective educational AR makes visible what is otherwise invisible or inaccessible, directly supporting a learning objective. The design principle is instructional necessity: AR should show information that cannot be effectively communicated through other, simpler means. Example: A chemistry AR app that lets students point their phone at a molecule model and see the 3D electron orbital shapes overlaid in real space. This is effective because electron orbitals are inherently 3D and difficult to understand from 2D diagrams. Contrast with an AR app that simply displays a pop-up text box when you point at a historical building — the text could be shown on a webpage and the AR adds novelty without instructional value.

rubric.key: Articulates the principle of instructional necessity
Contrasts with or explains ineffective AR use

questions.question: A nonprofit wants to deliver math tutoring to students in rural Kenya where most families have feature phones but limited data plans. Describe a mobile learning approach that would work within these constraints.
questions.answer: Design an SMS-based spaced practice system: students register with a free SMS, then receive daily math problems at scheduled times (e.g., 4 PM after school). Students reply with their answer, and the system sends immediate feedback with a brief explanation. Problems use adaptive difficulty based on the student's response history. Weekly summary SMS messages reinforce progress. This works because: SMS is available on all feature phones, requires no data plan (SMS costs are minimal or zero-rated in many markets), operates on any network, and the asynchronous format accommodates students' schedules. A USSD menu could provide additional practice on demand.

rubric.key: Proposes SMS or USSD-based solution appropriate to feature phones
Addresses data cost and connectivity constraints specifically
Incorporates learning science principles (spaced practice, feedback)

major_assignments.title: Mobile Learning Experience Design and Prototype
major_assignments.description: Design and build a mobile learning experience that leverages at least two mobile-specific affordances (e.g., location, camera, push notifications, touch gestures, accelerometer). The experience must address a real learning need for a defined audience. Deliver a functional prototype (no-code app, mobile-optimized web experience, or AR experience) and a design rationale document.
objectives: Design mobile-native learning that leverages device affordances
Build a functional prototype using no-code or low-code tools
Apply UX principles for mobile educational content
Justify design decisions with mobile learning research
deliverables: Functional mobile prototype (testable on a phone)
FRAME model analysis of the design

criteria: Leverages at least two mobile-specific affordances meaningfully
Mobile features serve learning objectives, not just novelty
Design would not work equally well on desktop
Functional and testable on a mobile device
Touch-friendly interface with appropriate target sizes
Clear learning objectives aligned to activities
Appropriate use of microlearning or other mobile-suited strategies
Feedback and assessment integrated into the experience
FRAME model analysis demonstrates deep understanding
Design decisions supported by mobile learning research
User testing conducted and insights documented

quizzes.description: Bi-weekly quizzes on mobile learning theory and design principles

lab_assignments.description: Weekly hands-on labs: mobile UX design, microlearning, AR, app building

major_assignments.description: Mobile learning experience design and prototype project

midterm_examination.description: Covers mobile learning theory, UX design, and microlearning

final_project.description: Extended mobile learning project with iterative testing and revision

course_policies.attendance: Lab sessions are mandatory for hands-on mobile development work. More than 2 unexcused absences will reduce the final grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work loses 5% per day for up to 5 days. Prototype deadlines are firm due to user testing scheduling.
course_policies.collaboration: User testing requires peer participation. All design and development work must be individually completed.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All designs and prototypes must be original. Third-party assets require proper attribution. Violations result in a zero and referral.
course_policies.technology_requirements: A smartphone (iOS or Android) for testing. Computer with internet access. Free accounts for Glide, Thunkable, or similar no-code platform.
